findings.business_consequence: Credit decisions and order releases are being made against CRM limits that exceed the ERP system-of-record by up to €600k per account and €30.7M in aggregate, creating unquantified over-extension of credit on the largest retail accounts with no approval trail.
findings.description:
An outer join of the SAP S/4HANA customer master against the SAP CRM export on customer_id shows that of 318 matched accounts, 267 have a mismatched credit_limit_eur and 228 have mismatched payment_terms. The summed absolute credit-limit divergence is €30,675,000. The largest single discrepancy is Carrefour France (FR001): ERP €1.8M vs CRM €2.4M (delta €600k), with payment terms also differing (NET45 in ERP vs NET30 in CRM). Boots UK, Tesco UK and E.Leclerc follow the same pattern (€350k, €350k, €300k deltas). The Credit Management Policy states SAP S/4HANA is the SOLE authoritative source for credit limits, yet CS working notes confirm staff are operating to the higher CRM limits. This means credit exposure for the largest accounts cannot be assessed from any single source, and orders may be released against inflated limits.

What the data shows:
- Accounts with mismatched credit_limit_eur (of 318 matched): 267
- Accounts with mismatched payment_terms: 228
- Aggregate absolute credit-limit divergence (EUR): 30,675,000
- Carrefour France ERP credit limit (EUR): 1,800,000
- Carrefour France CRM credit limit (EUR): 2,400,000
- Carrefour France largest single delta (EUR): 600,000
- Policy names ERP as sole authoritative source: ERP only (“Single system of record: SAP S/4HANA is the sole authoritative source for all customer credit limits and” — Credit Management Policy Opella Europe)
- AR review confirms Carrefour split limits: Carrefour FR (“at — CRM has EUR 2,400,000 and ERP has EUR 1,800,000. This is a material difference. The” — Accounts Receivable Review Notes Q4 2025)
- Policy does not define authoritative system in practice: unresolved (“record. Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative. As a result,” — Accounts Receivable Review Notes Q4 2025)

The Credit Management Policy nominally names S/4HANA as authoritative, but internal notes indicate the policy does not define which system is _

Carrefour France FR001 CRM 2400000 NET30
SAP S/4HANA is the sole authoritative source for all customer credit limits and
Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative.

findings.title: CRM and S/4HANA disagree on credit limits for 267 of 318 customers — €30.7M of aggregate divergence against the policy's sole system of record

findings.business_consequence: Two-thirds of order volume (€59.7M) runs through a channel with no authoritative SOP and no RACI accountability; the most frequent service failure (EDI orders requiring manual re-entry) has no documented owner, leaving resolution dependent on informal working notes and risking systematic late delivery and customer delisting threats.
findings.description:
The order-flow export shows EDI is the dominant channel: 5,667 of 8,420 orders (67.3% by count) worth €59,711,399 (66.8% of value). Yet the Order Management SOP explicitly states EDI 'is not covered by this version of the SOP', and the O2C RACI covers 'Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only', with EDI process steps 'not included in this version of the RACI'. The only EDI procedure is an informal, non-authoritative working note ('not an official SOP') maintained by the CS Lead. The consequence is visible in the escalation log: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required' is the single largest root cause at 34 of 142 cases (23.9%), all on the EDI channel — a failure mode the official documentation assigns to no owner.

What the data shows:
- EDI share of orders by count (pct): 67.3
- EDI order value (EUR): 59,711,399.53
- EDI share of order value (pct): 66.8
- Escalations: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required': 34
- That root cause as pct of all escalations: 23.9
- SOP excludes EDI: excluded (“Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) channel operations. EDI is not covered by this version of the SOP. EDI” — Order Management SOP Opella Europe)
- RACI covers only manual/email: manual/email only (“This RACI covers Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only. EDI channel” — O2C Process RACI Opella Europe)
- No RACI row for EDI: no owner (“- A RACI for EDI dispute resolution (there is no row in the O2C RACI for EDI).” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)
- EDI ~two-thirds of volume per CS notes: 67 (“purchase orders automatically — it accounts for around 67% of our total order volume.” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)

sources.quote: EDI 5667 orders, 67.3% of rows, €59,711,399.53, 66.8% of value
EDI is not covered by this version of the SOP.
This RACI covers Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only. EDI channel process steps are not included in this version of the RACI.
EDI order not processed — manual intervention required: 34 cases (23.9%)

findings.title: EDI is 67% of order volume (€59.7M) but is explicitly excluded from both the Order Management SOP and the O2C RACI — no documented owner for two-thirds of the business

findings.business_consequence: €12.4M of EDI orders are recorded as not fulfilled, the largest single failure bucket; combined with the absence of an EDI SOP/RACI, this drives repeated stockout and 'order not received' escalations and explicit customer delisting threats, putting both revenue and key retail relationships at risk.
findings.description:
Cross-tabulating channel against fulfilment_status in the order-flow export shows 1,196 EDI orders are NOT_FULFILLED, totalling €12,362,494 — by far the largest unfulfilled bucket. EDI's unfulfilled rate (1,196 of 5,667 EDI orders ≈ 21%) is materially higher than Manual (320 of 1,802 ≈ 18%) and far higher than Email (111 of 767 ≈ 14%). This is the data footprint of the documentation gap in F2: the dominant, undocumented channel is also the worst-performing one. The escalation log corroborates the mechanism — 'Retailer EDI portal shows accepted but order not in our system' and 'EDI connection timeout. Order queued for manual processing' — and the CS working notes prescribe manually re-entering orders, an unscaled manual workaround for the highest-volume channel.

What the data shows:
- Unfulfilled EDI orders (count): 1,196
- Unfulfilled EDI order value (EUR): 12,362,493.74
- Unfulfilled Manual orders (count): 320
- Unfulfilled Email orders (count): 111
- CS workaround is manual re-entry: manual re-entry (“Action: Manually re-enter the order while the EDI issue is investigated. Do not wait for” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)

_Flagged for review: Retitle to reflect the largest absolute bucket, not a 4x rate: e.g. 'EDI is the largest unfulfilled order bucket — 1,196 unfulfilled orders worth €12.4M.' State that EDI's unfulfilled rate (~21%) is modestly higher than Manual (~18%) and Email (~14%), not 4x. Replace causal language ('drives,' 'is the data footprint of') with associative phrasing (e.g., 'coincides with' / 'may be related to'). Lim_

current_state.domain_overview: Order-to-Cash covers how customer orders are received, credit-checked, fulfilled, invoiced and collected. Orders arrive through several channels, with electronic data interchange (EDI) carrying the largest share.

format_taxonomy.description: Row-per-transaction tabular data with typed columns (amounts, dates, account IDs, channel). Machine-readable and directly aggregatable.
format_taxonomy.examples: Order-flow export, customer-master extracts from the ERP and CRM, accounts-receivable ledger.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 1 — Structured transactional export

format_taxonomy.description: Prose documents that state the intended (to-be) rules: who approves what, which system is authoritative, and the documented procedure.
format_taxonomy.examples: Order Management SOP, Credit Policy, the Order-to-Cash process RACI.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 2 — Governance & policy document

format_taxonomy.description: Semi-structured incident and escalation records plus free-text agent notes — the lived reality of how exceptions are actually handled.
format_taxonomy.examples: Customer-service escalation log, EDI dispute-resolution working notes.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 3 — Operational log & working notes

process_detail.body: EDI orders flow automatically into order management across 14 connections (8 on the organisation's own platform, 6 still operated by the former parent). Telephone orders are keyed into the ERP within 30 minutes of the call; email orders within one business day, against per-market cut-off times.
process_detail.title: Order receipt

process_detail.body: A mandatory sequence runs in the ERP: customer account status, credit-limit verification against the outstanding balance, product availability at the allocated distribution centre, minimum-order-quantity compliance, and pricing validation (a variance under 2% is processed at the system price; 2% or more is referred to the account manager).
process_detail.title: Order validation

process_detail.body: The ERP places an order on credit hold automatically where it would take the account past its approved limit, or where an invoice is more than 60 days overdue. The agent notifies the Credit Controller within four business hours; release authority follows the approval-authority bands.
process_detail.title: Credit assessment

process_detail.body: Confirmation issues within four business hours for telephone orders and two for email orders. Released orders are picked, packed and dispatched from the market's distribution centre against the documented standard lead times.
process_detail.title: Order confirmation & fulfilment

process_detail.body: Invoices generate automatically on dispatch confirmation. Payment terms follow the account tier: major retail on NET 45 with a 1% early-payment discount, pharmacy and wholesale on NET 30, and new accounts on NET 14 or cash with order for the first six months.
process_detail.title: Invoicing

process_detail.body: Aged-debtor reporting runs monthly; balances over 60 days are reviewed individually. Collections follow the overdue escalation ladder, and the team completes a quarterly self-assessment of compliance.
process_detail.title: Accounts receivable & collections

process_flow.description: Orders arrive by EDI, manual entry, email or fax and are recorded for processing.
process_flow.name: Order receipt

process_flow.description: Each order is checked against the customer's credit limit before it is released.
process_flow.name: Credit check

process_flow.description: Released orders are picked, packed and dispatched.
process_flow.name: Fulfilment

process_flow.description: Dispatched orders are invoiced to the customer.
process_flow.name: Invoicing

process_flow.description: Outstanding invoices are tracked and collected.
process_flow.name: Collections

current_state.process_summary: Orders enter through EDI, manual entry, email and fax. Customer and credit information is held in two systems — SAP S/4HANA and SAP CRM. Orders are checked against a credit limit, released to the warehouse for fulfilment, then invoiced and collected by the accounts receivable team.

system_inventory.name: SAP S/4HANA
system_inventory.system_of_record_for: Customer credit limits (per the Credit Policy)

system_profiles.how_used: Customer Service, Credit Control, Finance and Accounts Receivable all work in S/4HANA: orders are recorded here, the credit check reads the credit limit held against the account, and invoices and open receivables are tracked here through to collection.
system_profiles.limitations: Holds its own credit limit per account. For 267 shared accounts that value differs from the one held in CRM, and the credit check reads whichever record the system resolves first.
system_profiles.name: SAP S/4HANA
system_profiles.owners: Owned by Finance Systems; used daily by Customer Service, Credit Control and Accounts Receivable.
system_profiles.role: The core ERP and the system of record for orders, credit limits, invoicing and collections.

system_profiles.how_used: Used by the commercial team to manage account relationships; it also holds credit limits and payment terms that were updated by hand over time.
system_profiles.limitations: CRM credit limits were edited manually without an approval trail. Its values differ from the ERP — Carrefour France shows €2.4M in CRM against €1.8M in the ERP, a €600,000 difference on a single account.
system_profiles.name: SAP CRM
system_profiles.owners: Owned by the commercial function; no shared governance with Finance Systems over the credit fields.
system_profiles.role: The customer-relationship system, holding account and commercial relationship data for the same retail customers.

system_profiles.how_used: Carries 67% of order volume (5,667 of 8,420 orders). Orders flow straight into order management for fulfilment; exceptions are picked up by hand by Customer Service.
system_profiles.limitations: Not covered by the Order Management SOP, and no credit check runs on it before release. 1,196 EDI orders (€12.4M) sit unfulfilled, and 'EDI order not processed' appears 34 times in the escalation log.
system_profiles.name: EDI / order-management channel
system_profiles.owners: No named owner — the Order-to-Cash RACI leaves EDI order processing and EDI dispute resolution as 'Not assigned'.
system_profiles.role: The electronic data interchange channel through which the largest share of customer orders arrives.

synthesis.dependency_notes: OPP3 depends on OPP1. OPP1, OPP2, OPP4 and OPP5 are independent of each other and can run in parallel.

evidence_register.data_point: 267 of 318 shared accounts hold a different credit limit; Carrefour France differs by €600,000.
The ERP is named the sole authoritative system for credit limits.
'Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative.'
Neither the SOP nor the RACI covers the EDI channel.
1,196 unfulfilled EDI orders (€12.4M); 34 'EDI not processed' escalations.
6 of 14 connections remain under the transitional service arrangement.
Governing service terms and a firm transfer date are not in the pack.
340 active accounts in the ERP versus 318 in the CRM — a 22-account difference.

executive_summary.headline: Order-to-Cash runs on two customer systems that disagree on credit, and a dominant electronic channel that no procedure owns — together putting material order value and revenue at risk.
executive_summary.opportunity: Reconcile the customer master to a single agreed source of truth, bring the electronic channel under documented ownership with automated exception handling, then add a real-time credit gate to the channel that needs it most.
executive_summary.situation: Orders arrive mainly through EDI, which carries two-thirds of volume yet sits outside the documented process and has no owner. Credit checks run against two systems whose limits diverge for 267 of 318 shared accounts.

metrics_framework.definition: Share of the 267 shared retail accounts holding a single agreed credit limit across the ERP and CRM, with an approval trail on any change.
metrics_framework.name: Customer-master agreement
metrics_framework.target: Every one of the 267 shared accounts reconciled, with no unapproved limit change thereafter.

metrics_framework.definition: Share of EDI orders fulfilled without a manual exception, against the 1,196 (€12.4M) not fulfilled today.
metrics_framework.name: EDI fulfilment rate
metrics_framework.target: A sustained, material reduction in unfulfilled EDI orders after go-live; the 1,196 baseline is the yardstick.

metrics_framework.definition: Customer-service escalations for 'EDI order not processed' — the single most frequent escalation today (34 cases).
metrics_framework.name: EDI escalation rate
metrics_framework.target: A clear downward trend against the 34-case baseline as exceptions resolve automatically.

metrics_framework.definition: Share of the EDI channel (67% of all orders) that receives a systematic credit check before release.
metrics_framework.name: EDI credit coverage
metrics_framework.target: Near-complete credit coverage of the EDI channel once the customer master is reconciled.

metrics_framework.definition: Share of automated credit assessments (approve/hold/flag) that a credit analyst confirms as correct on review.
metrics_framework.name: Credit-decision accuracy
metrics_framework.target: A high confirmation rate at go-live, improving through tuning; tracked against analyst review.

metrics_framework.definition: Share of the 14 live EDI connections operated on the organisation's own platform rather than the former parent's (8 of 14 today).
metrics_framework.name: EDI connection ownership
metrics_framework.target: All 14 connections on the organisation's own platform on the published migration schedule.

metrics_framework.definition: Whether the ERP and CRM hold the same active-account population (a 22-account difference today).
metrics_framework.name: Customer-master population alignment
metrics_framework.target: A single agreed active-account population across both systems, kept aligned at onboarding.

after_process.description: A guided session reviews the conflicting records; the platform surfaces every discrepancy and proposes the canonical value.
after_process.name: Reconciliation session

after_process.description: The teams confirm the authoritative credit limit per account.
after_process.name: Canonical limit agreed

after_process.description: A sync rule keeps the two systems aligned going forward.
after_process.name: Systems aligned

before_process.description: An order arrives for a major retail account.
before_process.name: Order arrives

before_process.description: The credit check uses whichever record the system resolves first.
failure_points: ERP and CRM hold different limits for the same account
No agreed source of truth, no approval trail on overrides
before_process.name: Credit limit looked up

before_process.description: The order is released, sometimes against an inflated limit.
before_process.name: Order released

business_impact.derivation: 267 shared accounts differ today; the largest single gap is Carrefour France at €600,000 (€2.4M in CRM vs €1.8M in ERP).
business_impact.narrative: Removes the credit-limit disagreement across the largest retail accounts and establishes a single agreed source of truth.

opportunities.data_readiness: high — both credit limits already exist as structured fields in the ERP and CRM; the discrepancy set (267 accounts) is computable directly from the two extracts.
opportunities.escalation: Where the credit and commercial teams disagree on the canonical value, or the gap is material (e.g. the €600,000 Carrefour France gap), the item is held for the Credit Controller to adjudicate rather than auto-resolved.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: The assistant presents the conflicting ERP and CRM values side by side for each of the 267 shared accounts, proposes the canonical limit with its reasoning, and records the human decision. It never changes a limit on its own — it prepares the decision and writes the agreed value back only after sign-off.
opportunities.implementation_approach: A human-in-the-loop reconciliation supported by an assistant that surfaces conflicts and proposes the canonical record; the credit team makes the final call.
opportunities.operational_readiness: high — Credit Control already owns credit decisions in the RACI, so there is a clear accountable owner to run the reconciliation.
opportunities.overview: Agree a single source of truth for customer credit limits and reconcile the ERP and CRM records, with the credit and commercial teams signing off the canonical values.
risks: The credit and commercial teams must agree the canonical-record rules
success_metrics: A single agreed credit limit per active retail account
An approval trail for any future limit change
opportunities.technical_complexity: medium — read-and-compare across two SAP systems with a governed write-back; no model judgement on the numbers themselves.
opportunities.title: Customer Master Reconciliation

after_process.description: Each exception is classified by type and auto-routed to the right resolution path.
after_process.name: Exception classified

after_process.description: Genuine ambiguities reach an agent; the rest resolve automatically.
after_process.name: Only ambiguities escalate

before_process.description: An EDI order fails or is flagged.
before_process.name: EDI exception raised

before_process.description: An agent classifies, routes and resolves it by hand.
before_process.name: Manual triage

business_impact.derivation: EDI orders not processed account for 34 of the customer-service escalations.
business_impact.narrative: Reduces the manual customer-service load from EDI exceptions, which are today the most frequent escalation.

opportunities.data_readiness: high — the order-flow export already carries the fulfilment status and channel needed to identify and type EDI exceptions.
opportunities.escalation: Any exception that does not match a known rule, or that the rule engine classifies with low confidence, is routed to a Customer Service agent with the classification and source order attached — nothing is silently dropped.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: Each EDI exception is classified by type against the documented rules and auto-routed to its resolution path. Clear-cut cases (the bulk of the 1,196 unfulfilled EDI orders) resolve without a person; the agent's queue holds only genuine ambiguities.
opportunities.implementation_approach: A deterministic, auditable rule engine on the EDI order stream; no AI judgement required.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — Customer Service handles these exceptions today, but EDI has no named owner in the RACI, so accountability for the new process must be assigned first.
opportunities.overview: Automate the triage of EDI order exceptions so only genuine ambiguities reach the customer-service team.
risks: Rule completeness — uncovered edge cases will still reach the team
success_metrics: Fewer manual customer-service interventions on EDI exceptions
opportunities.technical_complexity: low — a deterministic, auditable rule engine on the EDI stream; no AI judgement required, so it is the simplest to stand up.
opportunities.title: EDI Order Exception Handling

after_process.description: Each EDI order is assessed against the reconciled customer master and credit policy: approve, hold, or flag.
after_process.name: Real-time credit assessment

after_process.description: Flagged orders go to a credit analyst; approved orders proceed.
after_process.name: Analyst reviews flags

before_process.description: An EDI order is received on the dominant channel.
before_process.name: EDI order arrives

before_process.description: The order proceeds to fulfilment with no systematic credit check on the EDI channel.
failure_points: No credit gate on the channel carrying 67% of orders
before_process.name: Released without a credit gate

business_impact.derivation: EDI carries 67% of orders (5,667 of 8,420); today these are released without a systematic credit check.
business_impact.narrative: Brings a credit check to the EDI channel that carries 67% of orders, closing the coverage gap once the customer master is clean.

opportunities.data_readiness: medium — depends on the reconciled customer master from OPP1; until credit limits agree across the two systems, a real-time check cannot be trusted.
opportunities.escalation: Low-confidence assessments and every 'hold' are routed to a credit analyst with the order, the limit used and the reasoning, so the analyst makes the final call. The agent never releases a held order on its own.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: For each EDI order the agent reads the reconciled customer master and the Credit Policy and returns one of three outcomes — approve, hold, or flag — with the reasoning shown. Approved orders proceed to fulfilment; held and flagged orders wait for a human decision.
opportunities.implementation_approach: An AI agent on the EDI order stream that approves, holds or flags each order; escalates low-confidence cases to a human.
opportunities.operational_readiness: low — credit analysts must come to trust and act on the agent's flags, and the EDI channel has no named owner today; this needs the foundations (OPP1, EDI ownership) in place first.
opportunities.overview: Apply a real-time credit assessment to the EDI order stream so the channel that carries most of your orders is no longer released without a credit check.
risks: Needs the reconciled customer master (OPP1) first
Credit analysts must trust and act on the agent's flags
success_metrics: Credit assessment coverage of the EDI channel
Low rate of incorrectly held orders
opportunities.technical_complexity: high — real-time assessment on the live EDI stream against the ERP credit module, with model judgement that must be auditable.
opportunities.title: AI Credit Decisioning

after_process.description: Each connection is migrated onto the organisation's own EDI platform on the published schedule.
after_process.name: Connection migrated

after_process.description: Incidents are resolved under the organisation's own service levels, with no dependency on the former parent.
after_process.name: Service owned end to end

before_process.description: An EDI incident occurs on one of the six connections operated by the former parent.
before_process.name: Incident on a parent-run connection

before_process.description: Resolution depends on the parent's team under the transitional arrangement.
failure_points: No control over response times on these connections
Governing service terms are not in the organisation's hands
before_process.name: Resolution waits on the parent

business_impact.derivation: Six of the fourteen live connections remain under the transitional arrangement today.
business_impact.narrative: Removes the dependency on the former parent for the connections carrying several of the largest retail accounts.

opportunities.data_readiness: medium — the connection inventory and managing entity are documented, but the governing service terms sit in a schedule not included in the pack.
opportunities.escalation: Where a connection's service terms or migration date are unclear, the item is held for the EDI integration lead and the transition programme office rather than migrated blind.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: Each of the six connections is migrated to the organisation's own platform on the schedule and validated against live order flow before the parent's connection is retired. The programme touches one connection at a time so order flow is never interrupted.
opportunities.implementation_approach: A phased migration on the published schedule, one connection at a time, validated against live order flow before the parent's connection is decommissioned.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — the organisation owns eight connections already and has run two migrations, but the remaining six depend on the former parent's cooperation.
opportunities.overview: Bring the six EDI connections still operated by the former parent onto the organisation's own platform, so it controls the service end to end.
risks: The governing service terms are not in the document pack
Migration dates for the later connections are provisional
success_metrics: All connections operated on the organisation's own platform
Incident resolution under the organisation's own service levels
opportunities.technical_complexity: high — a live cutover of production EDI connections between two platforms, coordinated with an external party.
opportunities.title: EDI Connection Transition Programme

after_process.description: The two masters are compared and the missing accounts surfaced for review.
after_process.name: Populations compared

after_process.description: Missing records are created or retired under a maintained onboarding rule so the populations stay aligned.
after_process.name: Records aligned & governed

before_process.description: A new account is set up in the ERP.
before_process.name: Account created in the ERP

before_process.description: No matching record is maintained in the CRM.
failure_points: 22 accounts exist in the ERP with no CRM counterpart
Any process reading the CRM misses them
before_process.name: No matching CRM record

business_impact.derivation: The ERP holds 340 active accounts to the CRM's 318 — a 22-account difference.
business_impact.narrative: Aligns the account populations so the credit reconciliation works from a complete, agreed set of accounts.

opportunities.data_readiness: high — both account populations are structured fields; the 22-account difference is computable directly from the two extracts.
opportunities.escalation: Any account whose status is ambiguous (e.g. possibly inactive) is held for the data steward and the account manager to decide rather than auto-aligned.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: The platform lists the accounts present in one master but not the other and proposes a create-or-retire action for each; a data steward confirms every decision. It never creates or retires a record on its own.
opportunities.implementation_approach: A human-in-the-loop alignment: the platform surfaces the population difference and a data steward confirms each create-or-retire decision.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — the alignment needs a named data steward and a maintained onboarding rule, which do not exist today.
opportunities.overview: Align the two customer masters on which accounts exist, so the credit reconciliation has a clean, complete population to work from.
risks: Some ERP-only accounts may be legitimately inactive and need retiring, not adding
success_metrics: The two masters hold the same active-account population
A maintained rule keeps them aligned at onboarding
opportunities.technical_complexity: low — a compare-and-confirm over two master-data extracts.
opportunities.title: Customer Master Population Alignment

pain_points.business_consequence: Credit can be extended beyond the policy-approved limit, and the same account can be invoiced on two different payment terms.
pain_points.description: Your ERP and CRM hold different credit limits and payment terms for the same major retail accounts, with no single agreed source of truth.

detail_table.caption: Where the two systems disagree, account by account — limits and terms.
detail_table.title: Credit-limit discrepancy register — top accounts

pain_points.failure_pattern: Orders are released against whichever limit the system resolves first, so the same account can trade on two different limits.
pain_points.root_cause: Both systems were carried over at carve-out and were never reconciled; CRM limits were updated by hand without an approval trail.
pain_points.title: Two customer systems disagree on credit limits

pain_points.business_consequence: The channel carrying most of the order value runs with no documented procedure and no accountable owner.
pain_points.description: EDI carries most of your order volume, yet it is not covered by the Order Management SOP and has no owner in the Order-to-Cash RACI.

detail_table.caption: Where each governing document leaves the EDI channel.
rows: Lists telephone and email as the standard channels; EDI is out of scope.
Covers manual and email steps only; no EDI rows.
Informal notes, explicitly 'not an official SOP'.
detail_table.title: Document-level evidence

pain_points.failure_pattern: EDI orders that fail are handled informally, with no governed process or named owner.
pain_points.root_cause: The documented process was written for manual and email orders; EDI grew to dominate without the procedure or accountability catching up.
pain_points.title: Two-thirds of orders run on an undocumented channel

pain_points.business_consequence: The largest single block of unfulfilled orders — and the most frequent escalation — sits on the channel nobody owns.
pain_points.description: A large block of EDI orders is not fulfilled, on the same channel that has no documented process or owner.

detail_table.caption: Unfulfilled orders cluster on the EDI channel.
detail_table.title: Unfulfilled orders by channel

pain_points.failure_pattern: EDI orders not processed is the single most common customer-service escalation.
pain_points.root_cause: Without a governed EDI process, failures are absorbed reactively through manual re-entry rather than prevented.
pain_points.title: EDI order failures concentrate on the unowned channel

pain_points.business_consequence: The organisation cannot guarantee its own response times on the connections carrying several of its largest retail accounts.
pain_points.description: Six of the fourteen live EDI connections are still operated by the former parent under a transitional service arrangement, with the service terms held in a schedule that is not in the document pack.

detail_table.caption: The six connections to bring onto the organisation's own platform.
detail_table.note: The governing service terms sit in a schedule not included in the document pack; the migration dates are the register's stated targets.
detail_table.title: Connections still under the transitional arrangement

pain_points.failure_pattern: When one of these six connections has an incident, resolution depends on the parent's team and a transitional agreement the organisation does not control.
pain_points.root_cause: At carve-out, eight connections moved to the organisation's own platform while six remained on the parent's; the migration is partly complete.
pain_points.title: Six EDI connections still run on the former parent's platform

pain_points.business_consequence: A clean reconciliation cannot be completed until the account populations themselves are aligned.
pain_points.description: The ERP holds more customer accounts than the CRM, so the two systems do not even agree on which accounts exist, let alone their credit terms.

detail_table.caption: The two masters hold different account populations.
detail_table.title: Customer-master population

pain_points.failure_pattern: Twenty-two accounts exist in the ERP with no CRM counterpart, so any process that reads the CRM misses them entirely.

quantified.label: accounts in the ERP but not the CRM

pain_points.root_cause: Accounts were created in the ERP without a matching record being maintained in the CRM after carve-out.
pain_points.title: The two customer masters are not aligned on which accounts exist

risk_register.mitigation: Material gaps (e.g. the €600,000 Carrefour France gap) are held for the Credit Controller to adjudicate; nothing auto-resolves.
risk_register.risk: The credit and commercial teams cannot agree the canonical credit limit for a contested account.

risk_register.mitigation: Unmatched and low-confidence exceptions route to an agent with the source order attached; coverage is reviewed as new types appear.
risk_register.risk: The automated EDI exception rules do not cover an edge case, so it still reaches an agent.

risk_register.mitigation: Every hold and low-confidence case is shown with its reasoning and the limit used, so analysts can confirm rather than re-derive.
risk_register.risk: Credit analysts do not trust the automated credit assessment and override it by default.

risk_register.mitigation: Migrate one connection at a time on the published schedule, validating against live order flow before decommissioning.
risk_register.risk: A TSA connection's migration slips because the former parent's cooperation or service terms are unclear.

risk_register.mitigation: A data steward confirms each create-or-retire decision; ambiguous accounts are held for the account manager.
risk_register.risk: ERP-only accounts are added to the CRM when some should instead be retired as inactive.

items.rationale: Establishes the single source of truth the rest depends on.
items.title: Customer Master Reconciliation

items.rationale: Cuts the most frequent escalation; independent, can run now.
items.title: EDI Order Exception Handling

items.rationale: Aligns the account populations so the reconciliation is clean.
items.title: Customer Master Population Alignment

items.rationale: Credit-checks the dominant EDI channel; needs OPP1 first.
items.title: AI Credit Decisioning

items.rationale: Brings the six parent-run connections onto the own platform.
items.title: EDI Connection Transition Programme

items.rationale: Consolidate customer data onto a single platform.
items.title: CRM consolidation

items.rationale: Strengthen credit controls in the core ERP.
items.title: ERP credit module upgrade

synthesis.sequencing_rationale: Customer Master Reconciliation (OPP1) comes first because AI Credit Decisioning (OPP3) needs a clean, single credit limit per account to work. EDI Order Exception Handling (OPP2) and the population alignment (OPP5) are independent and run in parallel from the start; the connection transition (OPP4) follows on the published migration schedule.

source_index.what_we_read: your SAP S/4HANA Customer Master Export
your Accounts Receivable Review Notes Q4 2025

source_index.business_name: EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes
source_index.what_we_read: your EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes
your Retail Customer Onboarding Guide Europe
your Sanofi Consumer Healthcare O2C SOP 2023

synthesis.strategic_readiness: The current state can support the near-term moves once the customer master is reconciled; the credit decisioning step is the main capability to build toward.

strategy_profile.notes: Consolidate the inherited landscape while modernising for the EDI-dominated order reality.

synthesis.target_state: Order-to-Cash converges on one trusted customer master, with every credit limit agreed across both systems and changes governed by an approval trail. The electronic channel is owned, documented, and self-healing: routine exceptions resolve automatically and only genuine ambiguities reach a person. Every order — whatever the channel — passes a consistent credit check before release, so the volume that flows through EDI is no longer a blind spot.

traceability.expected_outcome: One agreed credit limit per account, with an approval trail.
A credit gate on the channel carrying most order value.
Routine EDI exceptions resolve without an agent.
All connections on the organisation's own platform.
traceability.summary: Six connections under the former parent

traceability.expected_outcome: One agreed active-account population across both systems.
